text: The slide visually demonstrates the port placement for the robotic median arcuate ligament release procedure.
The surgeon incises the gastrohepatic ligament, revealing the corral fibers running caudally over the aorta.
The surgeon creates exposure of the crural fibers, preparing to divide them over the aorta.
The surgeon screens the curl fibers to the left while peeling the gastrohepatic ligament off them, progressing cephalad during the dissection.
The surgeon continues the dissection cephalad, reaching under the stomach along the lesser curvature, exposing the posterior wall of the stomach.

The surgeon retracts the curl fibers to the left side of the screen, exposing the posterior gastric wall. The dissection progresses through the retrogastric tunnel towards the greater curve, aiming to encircle the gastroesophageal junction with a Penrose drain.
The surgeon performs a greater curvature dissection using a vessel sealer to transect the short gastric vessels, progressing towards the angle of His and the esophageal hiatus.
The surgeon is positioned high above the spleen, having divided the very short gastric vessels using a vessel sealer.

The surgeon uses a grasper to pass from the lesser curve to the greater curve, encircling the gastrohepatic ligament and gastroesophageal junction with a Penrose drain, retracting it toward the patient's left shoulder.

The surgeon incises the peritoneum over the gastrohepatic ligament to improve exposure of the crura, creating more laxity. Subsequently, they proceed to take down additional retrogastric attachments beneath the ligament.

The surgeon retracts the stomach, esophagus, and gastrohepatic ligament away from the curl fibers using a Penrose drain. Once the structures are adequately retracted, the dissection focuses on separating the curl fibers from the aorta.
The surgeon attempts to develop a plane between the right and left crura, aiming to expose the aortic adventitia.

The surgeon carefully dissects layer by layer, pushing the right cruce to the left side of the screen and the left cruce to the right side, gradually revealing the pearly white adventitia overlying the aorta.

The surgeon carefully dissects layer by layer, revealing the pulsating aorta as the crura are pushed aside. The dissection progresses down to the pearly white adventitia of the aorta, ensuring clear exposure of the vessel.

The surgeon carries the dissection caudad, dividing fibers of the left cruce as they cross over the aorta. As the dissection progresses, the fibers are divided until the origin of the celiac axis becomes visible.

The surgeon completes the dissection by dividing the remaining fibers of the left cruce and the median arcuate ligament, ensuring that the origin of the celiac axis is no longer compressed by surrounding tissue.
The surgeon begins to skeletonize the celiac artery, carefully removing loose areolar tissue from both sides to expose the vessel.

The diaphragmatic crura are fully mobilized, no longer compressing the celiac axis, and the median arcuate ligament tissue is completely divided, ensuring unobstructed blood flow through the celiac artery.
The surgeon continues to skeletonize the celiac artery, removing any remaining tissue overlying it as it exits the aorta, ensuring the artery is fully exposed.
The surgeon re-approximates the divided diaphragmatic crura above the celiac artery using interrupted figure-of-eight sutures with O-ethabons to prevent a hiatal hernia.
The surgeon removes the retractor and ports, marking the conclusion of the operation.
